name: Neodymium
name_origin: From Greek 'neos' (new) and 'didymos' (twin), the 'new twin' separated from didymium by Welsbach.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Relatively abundant lanthanide (~41.5 ppm in crust), about as common as copper; mined chiefly from monazite and bastnaesite, with major production in China.

compounds.name: Neodymium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Light blue-grey solid used as a glass colourant (violet-purple) and as a starting material for NdFeB magnet production.

compounds.name: Neodymium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Mauve-coloured hygroscopic salt used as a catalyst in polymerisation and as a precursor for neodymium magnets.

compounds.name: Neodymium(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: Used in fluoride glasses and in the production of metallic neodymium by electrolysis.

compounds.name: Neodymium iron boride
compounds.summary: Tetragonal intermetallic compound that forms the basis of the strongest commercial permanent magnets.

uses: Neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB) permanent magnets in motors, generators, hard drives, and headphones
Nd:YAG (Y3Al5O12:Nd3+) infrared lasers used in surgery, materials processing, and military rangefinders
Glass colourant producing violet-to-wine-red 'neodymium glass' tints
Welder's protective glasses (didymium) that filter sodium-D emission
Catalysts for polymerisation of butadiene and isoprene synthetic rubbers
reactivity_summary: Soft, silvery metal that tarnishes rapidly in air, forming a flaky oxide that promotes further corrosion. Reacts with water (slowly cold, quickly hot) and dissolves readily in dilute acids; burns readily when finely divided.
isotopes_summary: Neodymium has seven naturally occurring isotopes (Nd-142 to Nd-150); five are stable and two (Nd-144, Nd-150) are very long-lived primordial radioisotopes. Nd-142 is the most abundant (~27%).

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Neodymium
